attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old woman of African descent presents to the General Practice clinic concerned about multiple small, dark brown to black papules on her face that have been present for many years. The lesions do not bleed or itch but have gradually increased in number. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her parents have similar lesions on their faces but has never had them biopsied.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple 2-4 mm, well-circumscribed, hyperpigmented, dome-shaped papules distributed symmetrically across her cheeks and temples. The lesions are soft and non-tender, with no crusting or ulceration.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Punch biopsy of a representative lesion
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of dermatosis papulosa nigra (DPN), a common benign condition predominantly affecting people with darker skin types (fitzpatrick 4,5 and 6), particularly those of African or Asian descent. The characteristic appearance of multiple small, dark, dome-shaped papules on the face, along with the chronic, asymptomatic nature of the condition, makes this a straightforward clinical diagnosis.

Clinical diagnosis and reassurance is the most appropriate management as DPN has such characteristic features that further investigation is usually unnecessary. The patient should be reassured about the benign nature of the condition and informed that while the lesions may increase in number with age, they pose no health risk.

Punch biopsy would be unnecessarily invasive and is not indicated for typical appearing lesions. Biopsy would only be considered if there were atypical features or diagnostic uncertainty.

Dermoscopic examination and photography may be helpful in monitoring changes in pigmented lesions where there is concern about melanoma, but is not necessary for typical DPN lesions where the diagnosis is clear on clinical examination. They do not have malignant potential. The lesions of DPN are histologically similar to seborrheic keratoses, showing acanthosis and hyperkeratosis with pigmentation throughout the epidermis.

Referral to dermatologist is not necessary for diagnosis of typical DPN. Referral would only be appropriate if the patient desired treatment for cosmetic reasons or if there were atypical features raising concern for other diagnoses.

Skin scraping for fungal microscopy would not be helpful as DPN is not a fungal condition. The clinical appearance is not consistent with tinea or other fungal infections.

While treatment options such as electrodessication, cryotherapy, or shave excision exist for cosmetic concerns, many patients choose observation once reassured about the benign nature of the condition.
learningPoints: Dermatosis papulosa nigra is a benign condition that predominantly affects people with darker skin types.
Biopsy is not routinely indicated for typical appearing lesions of dermatosis papulosa nigra.
Clinical examination is usually sufficient for diagnosis of dermatosis papulosa nigra.
references: Duffill M. Dermatosis papulosa nigra. DermNet NZ. 2008.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/dermatosis-papulosa-nigra

Metin S, Lee B, Lambert W, Parish L. Dermatosis papulosa nigra: a clinically and histopathologically distinct entity. Clinics in Dermatology. 2017: 35 (5).  Availabe at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0738081X17300962
